topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped stone. I stared at the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the mouse. "Regretfully, your application for the merit scholarship has not been successful at this time." The words swam before my eyes, meaningless and sharp. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing loudly in the small dorm room. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated through my temples. I stood up, pacing the cramped space, kicking at the worn rug. Every shadow seemed to stretch and twist, reflecting the churning knot in my stomach. I needed to be angry, to channel this suffocating disappointment into something sharp and usable. I grabbed my backpack, the weight suddenly feeling like a leaden anchor, and started walking toward the door, every step deliberate and stiff.

[story 2]

"Denied." The single word felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I didn't even bother to read the rest of the notification; the rejection was already etched onto my face, a hard mask I quickly tried to perfect. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails bit into my palms. I turned around, glaring at the empty hallway, as if the very walls were conspiring against me. A tremor ran through my hands, a restless energy that wouldn't settle. I walked back to my desk, not to look at my notes, but to violently shove them aside. I picked up a pen, its plastic cool against my skin, and began scribbling furious, illegible lines across a scrap of paper, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. I wanted to scream, to shatter something, but all that escaped was a low, vibrating sound deep in my chest.

[story 3]

I reread the rejection letter three times, each reading bringing a fresh wave of icy frustration. The official language—'lacking in requisite metrics,' 'insufficient alignment'—felt like deliberate mockery. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, ragged breath that tasted like ash. My gaze swept over the room, landing on the framed photo of my family, and for a moment, I felt a profound, burning resentment toward everything that had led to this outcome. I pushed my chair back with unnecessary force, the scrape of metal against the floor sounding accusatory. I started pacing the length of the room again, faster this time, my shoulders hunched high around my ears, radiating a palpable sense of being wronged. I needed to prove that this setback wouldn't define me; I needed to burn the disappointment down to the foundation.
